data.title: Companies Like DiaMedica Therapeutics (NASDAQ:DMAC) Are In A Position To Invest In Growth
data.description:
We can readily understand why investors are attracted to unprofitable companies. For example, although Amazon.com made losses for many years after listing, if you had bought and held the shares since 1999, you would have made a fortune. But the harsh reality is that very many loss making companies burn through all their cash and go bankrupt.

So, the natural question for DiaMedica Therapeutics (NASDAQ:DMAC) shareholders is whether they should be concerned by its rate of cash burn. For the purpose of this article, we'll define cash burn as the amount of cash the company is spending each year to fund its growth (also called its negative free cash flow). Let's start with an examination of the business' cash, relative to its cash burn.

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

Advertisement

When Might DiaMedica Therapeutics Run Out Of Money?

A company's cash runway is the amount of time it would take to burn through its cash reserves at its current cash burn rate. In December 2025, DiaMedica Therapeutics had US$60m in cash, and was debt-free. In the last year, its cash burn was US$29m. So it had a cash runway of about 2.1 years from December 2025. Arguably, that's a prudent and sensible length of runway to have. The image below shows how its cash balance has been changing over the last few years.

Check out our latest analysis for DiaMedica Therapeutics

How Is DiaMedica Therapeutics' Cash Burn Changing Over Time?

DiaMedica Therapeutics didn't record any revenue over the last year, indicating that it's an early stage company still developing its business. Nonetheless, we can still examine its cash burn trajectory as part of our assessment of its cash burn situation. With the cash burn rate up 32% in the last year, it seems that the company is ratcheting up investment in the business over time. That's not necessarily a bad thing, but investors should be mindful of the fact that will shorten the cash runway. While the past is always worth studying, it is the future that matters most of all. So you might want to take a peek at how much the company is expected to grow in the next few years.

How Easily Can DiaMedica Therapeutics Raise Cash?

While DiaMedica Therapeutics does have a solid cash runway, its cash burn trajectory may have some shareholders thinking ahead to when the company may need to raise more cash. Issuing new shares, or taking on debt, are the most common ways for a listed company to raise more money for its business. Many companies end up issuing new shares to fund future growth. By looking at a company's cash burn relative to its market capitalisation, we gain insight on how much shareholders would be diluted if the company needed to raise enough cash to cover another year's cash burn.

Since it has a market capitalisation of US$357m, DiaMedica Therapeutics' US$29m in cash burn equates to about 8.2% of its market value. Given that is a rather small percentage, it would probably be really easy for the company to fund another year's growth by issuing some new shares to investors, or even by taking out a loan.

Is DiaMedica Therapeutics' Cash Burn A Worry?

Even though its increasing cash burn makes us a little nervous, we are compelled to mention that we thought DiaMedica Therapeutics' cash burn relative to its market cap was relatively promising. Considering all the factors discussed in this article, we're not overly concerned about the company's cash burn, although we do think shareholders should keep an eye on how it develops. data.title: Can Lexaria Bioscience (NASDAQ:LEXX) Afford To Invest In Growth?
data.description:
There's no doubt that money can be made by owning shares of unprofitable businesses. For example, although software-as-a-service business Salesforce.com lost money for years while it grew recurring revenue, if you held shares since 2005, you'd have done very well indeed. But while history lauds those rare successes, those that fail are often forgotten; who remembers Pets.com?

So, the natural question for Lexaria Bioscience (NASDAQ:LEXX) shareholders is whether they should be concerned by its rate of cash burn. In this article, we define cash burn as its annual (negative) free cash flow, which is the amount of money a company spends each year to fund its growth. First, we'll determine its cash runway by comparing its cash burn with its cash reserves.

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

Advertisement

Does Lexaria Bioscience Have A Long Cash Runway?

You can calculate a company's cash runway by dividing the amount of cash it has by the rate at which it is spending that cash. As at February 2026, Lexaria Bioscience had cash of US$5.3m and no debt. In the last year, its cash burn was US$9.5m. That means it had a cash runway of around 7 months as of February 2026. To be frank, this kind of short runway puts us on edge, as it indicates the company must reduce its cash burn significantly, or else raise cash imminently. You can see how its cash balance has changed over time in the image below.

Check out our latest analysis for Lexaria Bioscience

How Is Lexaria Bioscience's Cash Burn Changing Over Time?

In our view, Lexaria Bioscience doesn't yet produce significant amounts of operating revenue, since it reported just US$368k in the last twelve months. As a result, we think it's a bit early to focus on the revenue growth, so we'll limit ourselves to looking at how the cash burn is changing over time. Over the last year its cash burn actually increased by 26%, which suggests that management are increasing investment in future growth, but not too quickly. However, the company's true cash runway will therefore be shorter than suggested above, if spending continues to increase. While the past is always worth studying, it is the future that matters most of all. So you might want to take a peek at how much the company is expected to grow in the next few years.

How Hard Would It Be For Lexaria Bioscience To Raise More Cash For Growth?

Given its cash burn trajectory, Lexaria Bioscience shareholders should already be thinking about how easy it might be for it to raise further cash in the future. Companies can raise capital through either debt or equity. Many companies end up issuing new shares to fund future growth. By comparing a company's annual cash burn to its total market capitalisation, we can estimate roughly how many shares it would have to issue in order to run the company for another year (at the same burn rate).

Lexaria Bioscience's cash burn of US$9.5m is about 39% of its US$24m market capitalisation. That's not insignificant, and if the company had to sell enough shares to fund another year's growth at the current share price, you'd likely witness fairly costly dilution.

So, Should We Worry About Lexaria Bioscience's Cash Burn?

We must admit that we don't think Lexaria Bioscience is in a very strong position, when it comes to its cash burn. While its increasing cash burn wasn't too bad, its cash runway does leave us rather nervous. Once we consider the metrics mentioned in this article together, we're left with very little confidence in the company's ability to manage its cash burn, and we think it will probably need more money. Approach 1: Atmus Filtration Technologies Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes forecasts of a company’s future cash flows and discounts them back to today using a required rate of return. The idea is simple: what matters for you as a shareholder is the cash the business can return over time, expressed in today’s dollars.

For Atmus Filtration Technologies, Simply Wall St uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model based on projected Free Cash Flow (FCF) in $. The latest twelve months FCF is about $151.0 million. Analyst estimates extend out to 2027, with FCF for that year projected at $251.4 million. Beyond that, Simply Wall St extrapolates ten year FCF projections, with discounted values provided for each year from 2026 through 2035.

Adding those discounted cash flows and a terminal value together gives an estimated intrinsic value of about $71.08 per share. Versus the recent share price of $63.38, this implies a 10.8% discount, suggesting the stock is currently priced below this DCF estimate.

For a profitable company, the P/E ratio is a useful way to think about what you are paying for each dollar of earnings. It lets you compare different businesses on a like for like basis and it is widely used across the Machinery industry.

What counts as a “fair” P/E ratio often reflects the earnings growth investors expect and how risky they think those earnings are. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can support a higher P/E, while lower growth expectations or higher risk tend to line up with a lower multiple.

data.title: Here's Why We Think Lifco (STO:LIFCO B) Might Deserve Your Attention Today
data.description:
It's common for many investors, especially those who are inexperienced, to buy shares in companies with a good story even if these companies are loss-making. Sometimes these stories can cloud the minds of investors, leading them to invest with their emotions rather than on the merit of good company fundamentals. Loss making companies can act like a sponge for capital - so investors should be cautious that they're not throwing good money after bad.

So if this idea of high risk and high reward doesn't suit, you might be more interested in profitable, growing companies, like Lifco (STO:LIFCO B). Now this is not to say that the company presents the best investment opportunity around, but profitability is a key component to success in business.

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

Advertisement

Lifco's Earnings Per Share Are Growing

If you believe that markets are even vaguely efficient, then over the long term you'd expect a company's share price to follow its earnings per share (EPS) outcomes. That means EPS growth is considered a real positive by most successful long-term investors. Over the last three years, Lifco has grown EPS by 9.3% per year. That's a good rate of growth, if it can be sustained.

Careful consideration of revenue growth and earnings before interest and taxation (EBIT) margins can help inform a view on the sustainability of the recent profit growth. Lifco maintained stable EBIT margins over the last year, all while growing revenue 8.1% to kr28b. That's progress.

You can take a look at the company's revenue and earnings growth trend, in the chart below. To see the actual numbers, click on the chart.

Check out our latest analysis for Lifco

Fortunately, we've got access to analyst forecasts of Lifco's future profits. You can do your own forecasts without looking, or you can take a peek at what the professionals are predicting.

Are Lifco Insiders Aligned With All Shareholders?

Investors are always searching for a vote of confidence in the companies they hold and insider buying is one of the key indicators for optimism on the market. This view is based on the possibility that stock purchases signal bullishness on behalf of the buyer. Of course, we can never be sure what insiders are thinking, we can only judge their actions.

The real kicker here is that Lifco insiders spent a staggering kr44m on acquiring shares in just one year, without single share being sold in the meantime. The shareholders within the general public should find themselves expectant and certainly hopeful, that this large outlay signals prescient optimism for the business. We also note that it was the CEO, President & Director, Per Waldemarson, who made the biggest single acquisition, paying kr15m for shares at about kr301 each.

On top of the insider buying, we can also see that Lifco insiders own a large chunk of the company. Indeed, with a collective holding of 51%, company insiders are in control and have plenty of capital behind the venture. Intuition will tell you this is a good sign because it suggests they will be incentivised to build value for shareholders over the long term. Is Lifco Worth Keeping An Eye On?

One important encouraging feature of Lifco is that it is growing profits. Better yet, insiders are significant shareholders, and have been buying more shares. These factors alone make the company an interesting prospect for your watchlist, as well as continuing research. Don't forget that there may still be risks. data.title: Chemung Financial (CHMG) Net Margin Compression Reinforces Bearish Earnings Narratives
data.description:
Chemung Financial (CHMG) opened the new reporting year with Q1 2026 results that sit against a recent run of quarterly revenue between US$23.7 million and US$29.4 million and basic EPS that has ranged from a profit of about US$1.63 to a loss of US$1.34. Over the past few quarters, the company has seen total revenue move from US$23.7 million in Q3 2024 to US$25.3 million in Q4 2024 and then to US$29.4 million in Q4 2025, while basic EPS shifted from US$1.20 to US$1.29 and then to US$1.63 over the same periods, setting the backdrop for how investors judge the latest quarter. With a trailing net profit margin of 16.7% and earnings growth concerns already in focus, the fresh numbers land in a context where compression in profitability is a key part of the story.

See our full analysis for Chemung Financial.

With the headline figures in place, the next step is to weigh them against the most common narratives around Chemung Financial to see which storylines hold up and which ones the latest earnings call into question.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

Loan book climbs toward US$2.3b

  • Total loans moved from US$2,028.9 million in Q3 2024 to US$2,269.6 million by Q4 2025, with non performing loans in that period ranging between US$7.8 million and US$10.5 million.
  • What stands out for a bullish view is that higher loan balances sit alongside lower non performing loans over the last twelve months. Loans were at US$2,269.6 million and non performing loans at US$7.9 million in Q4 2025, which investors can compare with earlier quarters where loans were smaller and non performing balances reached US$10.5 million.

Margins and costs find a middle ground

  • On a trailing twelve month basis, net interest margin was 3.26% and the cost to income ratio was 63%. This compares with earlier trailing data points that show net interest margin at 2.76% and cost to income at 68.89% in Q4 2024.
  • Bears focus on the 9.5% annual decline in trailing earnings and the net profit margin moving from 24.3% to 16.7%. However, the improvement in net interest margin from 2.76% to 3.26% and the lower cost to income ratio at 63% suggest operating spread and efficiency were not moving in the same direction as headline profitability, which gives you a more mixed picture than a simple earnings decline story.
    • The 16.7% net profit margin over the last year still leaves room above many low margin businesses, even if it is below the prior 24.3% level that bears highlight.
    • Cost to income moving from 68.89% to 63% on a trailing basis shows expenses consumed a smaller share of income, which partially offsets the bearish point about weaker bottom line trends.

Rich P/E meets discounted DCF fair value

  • The shares trade at US$62.82 with a P/E of 20x, compared with a DCF fair value of US$95.39 and a sector context where the US Banks industry sits at 11.9x P/E and peers at 15.3x.
  • What creates tension for both bullish and bearish views is that the DCF fair value suggests about a 34.1% gap between price and that estimate, while the 20x P/E is above industry and peer levels. Investors weighing the 2.16% dividend yield and the longer term 9.5% annual earnings decline have to decide whether the discounted DCF fair value or the richer earnings multiple is more important for their own valuation work.
    • Bears point to the higher 20x P/E versus 11.9x for the broader US Banks industry as a sign the stock is not cheap on simple multiples, even with earnings under pressure.
    • Bulls counter that a 34.1% discount to the DCF fair value and ongoing dividends at a 2.16% yield provide some support, even if earnings and margins have come down from earlier levels.
